data.title: Oil Climbs Further as U.S.-Iran Peace Efforts Remain Stalled
data.description:
By Dow Jones Newswires Staff

Oil rose for a fifth-straight day as the Strait of Hormuz remained shuttered and neither the U.S. nor Iran signaled a return to peace talks in the near term.

President Trump said he wouldn't be rushed to end the war and announced that Israel and Lebanon had agreed a three-week extension to their cease-fire.

Front-month Brent crude oil was on track for its largest weekly gain since the first week of the conflict in early European trade. U.S. technology stock futures pushed higher even as government bond yields rose across the globe, as tech investors remained undaunted by the conflict.

  • Brent futures for June delivery rose 0.6% to $105.73 a barrel, while WTI is up 0.4% to $96.20 a barrel. "The longer the Strait remains shut, the greater the oil shock and the more distant the idea of Brent crude trading back to levels near $80 or below," analysts at ING said.
  • U.S. stock futures were mixed. Futures tied to the Dow Jones Industrial Average were 0.2% lower, while the S&P 500 rose 0.1%. The tech-heavy Nasdaq gained 0.6% premarket.

Chip maker Intel shares surged 22% in premarket trade after announcing a jump in sales on increased demand from agentic artificial-intelligence providers . Procter & Gamble is among companies set to announce earnings ahead of market open Friday.

  • Asia markets were mixed. South Korea's Kospi ended flat while Japan's Nikkei Stock Average was up 1.0% to end at a record high, led by chip-related stocks. Taiwan's Taiex was up 3.2%, led by gains in TSMC after regulators eased the limit on how much funds can invest in a single stock.

The Hang Seng Index rose 0.2% higher while China's Shanghai Composite closed 0.3% lower. Chip stocks surged in China and Hong Kong too after DeepSeek launched its long-awaited AI model update, boosting hopes for localization efforts.

  • European equity indexes mostly fell in opening trade. Banks and industrial stocks slid as the Europe-wide Stoxx 600 dropped 0.5%. London's FTSE 100 lost 0.4%, dragged by faltering defense stocks as Rolls-Royce and BAE Systems fell 2.3% and 1.9%, respectively. The French CAC 40 was down 0.55% as Safran fell 2.7%.

Germany's DAX nudged up 0.1%, buoyed by a 5.6% jump for software group SAP--the largest stock in the index--following earnings. Banks dragged both the Italian FTSE MIB and Spanish IBEX 35, as the indexes fell 0.5% and 0.9%, respectively. The Dutch AEX was 0.2% higher.

  • The dollar rose. Uncertainty around the Middle East war kept investors cautious and leaves oil prices elevated, supporting the dollar as a safe haven and America's position as a net oil exporter. The DXY dollar index climbed 0.1% to 98.865.
  • U.S. Treasury yields edged higher. "U.S. Treasurys sold off a touch as oil drifted higher despite the ongoing cease-fire," Barclays' Anshul Pradhan and Demi Hu said in a note. The two-year Treasury yield rose 1.7 basis points to 3.841%, while the 10-year yield was up 0.7 basis points to 4.329%, according to Tradeweb.
  • Eurozone government bond yields also rose. Near-term focus is on the European Central Bank's meeting next week, where interest rates are highly likely to be left unchanged. "The central bank thus gains time to carefully analyze the latest macroeconomic data and geopolitical developments--particularly in connection with the Middle East conflict--and weigh them against its previous projections," said BayernInvest's CIO Daniel Kerbach in a note. The 10-year German Bund yield gained 2.1 basis points to 3.028%.
  • Bitcoin fell 0.1% to $77,837.
  • In early European trading, New York gold futures are down 0.6% to $4,698.30 a troy ounce.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: Norway should use wealth fund for aid due to oil price rise in Iran war, Egeland says
data.description:
Key points:
  • Urges Norway to aid Iran war victims using wealth fund income
  • Government and fund CEO note losses from war
  • Norway is Western Europe's largest crude producer

By Gwladys Fouche

Norway should use some of the income from its $2.2 trillion sovereign wealth fund, the world's largest, to help civilians suffering from the war in Iran, like it did for Ukraine, the head of the Norwegian Refugee Council aid group said.

The NRC is one of the world's leading non-governmental aid organisations focused on displacement.

After Moscow's 2022 full-scale invasion of Ukraine, Norway set up a long-term aid plan for Kyiv, with the backing of all parties in parliament, amounting to some $28 billion between 2023 and 2030, among Ukraine's most generous backers per capita.

Jan Egeland, who was the top U.N. humanitarian official from 2003 to 2006 and a deputy Norwegian foreign minister in the 1990s, said Norway should do the same in the wake of the Iran war.

"Norway has now given enormous sums to Ukraine. And one of the arguments is that 'we earned a lot of money on oil and gas prices going through the roof. Why not say the same here?'," he told foreign reporters at NRC headquarters in Oslo.

"Let's give to the Lebanese and the Iranians and the other victims of this war, direct and indirect, because the oil prices are now $100 per barrel."

WEALTH FUND LOSS

Norway is Europe's largest gas supplier and Western Europe's largest crude producer. Egeland did not give details of how such a mechanism could potentially work.

Norwegian officials are sensitive to any criticism that the country benefits from conflicts that lead to increases in energy prices, given its self-image as a nation prioritising global governance such as by backing the U.N. or by being a major international aid donor.

Finance Minister Jens Stoltenberg has said it would be wrong to assume Norway was gaining from the war.

He has said that its export-led economy benefits most from a stable, peaceful international world order and a fall in global stocks damages the overall value of its wealth fund.

The Norwegian government also has never said it is giving money to Ukraine because it earned extra income from higher gas prices - estimated to be 1,270 billion crowns ($136.07 billion) in 2022 and 2023 according to finance ministry calculations.

Sovereign wealth fund CEO Nicolai Tangen told Reuters last month any benefit to the fund from higher petroleum revenues due to the Iran war is lower than the effects of lower share prices abroad and a strengthening of the crown currency.

In the first quarter, the fund reported a loss of 636 billion crowns as the Iran war weighed on global stocks.

The wealth fund declined to give additional comment, saying this was a question for Norwegian politicians. The finance ministry did not immediately reply to a request for comment.

($1 = 9.3334 Norwegian crowns)

data.title: Commerzbank plans further job cuts as part of updated strategy, Handelsblatt reports
data.description:
Commerzbank is planning further job cuts as part of its updated strategy as Italy's UniCredit escalates its advances for a takeover, Handelsblatt reported on Friday, citing sources familiar with the matter.

The extent of additional job cuts at the German bank has not yet been decided, the paper said.

Commerzbank declined to comment on the report but said it would provide details on its strategy on May 8.

Commerzbank has carried out two rounds of staff reductions in recent years.

data.title: German 10-Year Bund Yield Edges Higher, Euro Stable After Ifo Data — Market Talk
data.description: German 10-year Bund yields edge higher and the euro is little changed after Germany's Ifo data. The business climate index was worse than expected while expectations weakened in April. The Ifo sentiment index was 84.4 in April, below analysts' forecasts of 85.5 in the Wall Street Journal's poll and also below March's 86.3. The 10-year Bund yield trades at 3.037%, up 3 basis points after the data, having traded at 3.032% beforehand, according to Tradeweb. The euro remains steady against the dollar at $1.1688, little changed after the data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

data.title: Electrolux Shares Plunge on Net Loss, Nearly $1 Billion Share Issue
data.description:
By Nina Kienle

Shares in Electrolux fell sharply after the company said it swung to a net loss in the first quarter and launched a 9 billion Swedish kronor ($971.5 million) rights issue as North American sales take a hit.

The home-appliance manufacturer on Friday booked a net loss of 470 million Swedish kronor over the quarter, while revenue fell to 29.54 billion kronor from 32.58 billion kronor in the prior-year period. The company said it is facing a challenging U.S. pricing environment due to costs related to trade tariffs.

The figures were notably more negative than analysts' forecasts of quarterly net profit at 138.2 million kronor and revenue at 30.64 billion kronor, according to Visible Alpha estimates.

In European morning trade, shares fell 23% at 46.63 kronor to a 17-year low. Shares trade 27% lower over the year to date.

While Electrolux strengthened its market position in Europe and Brazil, North America saw organic sales decline 12%, reflecting weaker market conditions, in addition to increased tariff costs and a significant slowdown in market demand, it said.

Late Thursday Electrolux said that it had entered into agreements with China's Midea Group to support performance in North America, aiming to strengthen the group's product offering in food preservation and fabric care.

The Swedish manufacturer meanwhile said it would launch a 9 billion kronor rights issue, inviting shareholders to buy new shares at a discount. Proceeds will be used to partly fund the joint venture with Midea and carry out other restructuring measures across operations.

Electrolux has been grappling with subdued consumer demand and competition from lower-priced rivals, and is implementing cost-efficiency measures. The company is on track to reach its cost efficiency outlook of 3.5 billion to 4.0 billion kronor, it said.

Both the weak quarterly results and the rights issue are likely to weigh on the stock, SB1 Markets analysts said in a note.

"But the strategic measures in North America and the potential for a structural deal support a positive view in the medium term," they said.

The company backed its overall view for 2026 despite expected additional costs related to extended U.S. import tariffs on products that contain steel and aluminum.

Write to Nina Kienle at nina.kienle@wsj.com

data.title: ZAWYA: Exclusive: Egypt’s Korra Energi plans to float 11% stake on EGX
data.description:
Safia Mounir

Korra Energi, a private Egyptian energy and investment company, plans an initial public offering of 11% of its shares on the Egyptian Exchange, the company’s CEO Ayman Korra told Zawya Arabic.

The company this week appointed Prime Capital to promote and manage the planned offering but did not disclose the percentage of shares involved.

The company had previously said it plans an IPO of 20% of its shares during the first half of 2025, and the Egyptian Exchange announced the company’s provisional listing in preparation for the offering in December 2024, but completion of regulatory procedures delayed the process.

“We contracted Prime Holding as the offering manager… we will offer 11% of the company,” Korra said, noting that the timing of the subscription will be set after approval of the prospectus by the Financial Regulatory Authority.

Korra expects the private placement to account for 60% of the offered shares, with the remaining 40% allocated to the public offering.

Korra was founded in 1997 and operates in energy efficiency solutions and contracting services. Its issued capital stands at 450 million Egyptian pounds ($8.6 million).

Click here to read the article in Arabic.

data.title: Mondi Shares Tumble as Pricing Pressure Weighs on Outlook Amid Middle East Conflict
data.description:
By Najat Kantouar

Shares in Mondi fell, leading the FTSE 100 fallers, after the company warned that challenging market conditions continued in the first quarter and are expected to persist into the second quarter as price pressures increased amid the Middle East Conflict.

The London-listed paper-and-packaging company said rising energy, raw material and logistics costs, driven by the Middle East tensions, have further increased volatility, despite limited direct exposure to the region.

"We are actively responding with pricing actions. While there is a customary lag, we expect the impact of these price increases to take full effect in the third quarter of this year," Mondi said.

In early morning trading shares were down 6.9% at 784 pence. Year to date, shares have fallen about 13%.

For the first quarter, underlying earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization came in at 212 million euros ($247.7 million), which included 8 million euros of forestry fair value gain.

On a sequential basis, corrugated packaging and flexible packaging segments saw higher sales volumes, but this was offset by lower average selling prices, and later in the quarter, increased energy-related input costs, it said.

Meanwhile, the corrugated solutions and paper bags divisions faced margin pressure, while consumer flexibles unit's performance was broadly in line with the previous period, it said.

For 2026, the company expects no forestry fair value gain, reflecting recent reduction in wood prices in South Africa, and assuming no major change in market conditions for the rest of the year. Cost control and margin management remain a key focus, it said.

In April, Mondi announced the closure of a further three converting plants, including a consumer flexibles plant in Hungary and corrugated solutions plants in Poland and Germany.

These closures will reduce headcount by 450 during the year, it said. This brings the total number of recently announced plant closures to six, with customers being moved to other plants in the network, it said.

Write to Najat Kantouar at najat.kantouar@wsj.com

data.title: Nomura can afford to be more ambitious
data.description:
By Una Galani

The $26 bln bank is catching up with Japan’s reflation and is now trading at book value. Investor worries about it stumbling into another embarrassing drama are fading. That gives CEO Kentaro Okuda a chance to propel the stock further by juicing the return on equity target.

Full view will be published shortly.

Follow Una Galani on Linkedin and X.

CONTEXT NEWS

Nomura Holdings, Japan’s biggest brokerage and investment bank, on April 24 reported a net profit of 73.9 billion yen ($462.8 million) for the three months to the end of March, a 3% increase from the same period in the previous year.

data.title: AJ Bell Still Attractive to Investors Despite Higher Stock Price — Market Talk
data.description: Although AJ Bell's stock price has climbed recently it is still a solid deal for long-term investors, Deutsche Bank analyst David McCann says in a note. The U.K. investment platform's earnings are expected to grow by a steady 14% every year for the next four years. This means the stock will actually look cheaper in a few years compared to the profits it will be generating, McCann says. "We think AJ Bell still broadly offers a growth at reasonable price investment case, given FY 2025-2029 earnings per share compound annual growth rate of around 14%, and with the price to earnings ratio falling to the low teens on our outer-year forecasts," McCann says. Shares are down 2.2% at 530 pence. (anthony.orunagoriainoff@dowjones.com)

data.title: Mondi shares slump on Q1 earnings, Iran volatility warning
data.description:
The company said it expected the impact of the increases to take full effect in the third quarter of this year.

It also reported a sharp fall in first quarter underlying earnings as market conditions remained “challenging” and had move to cut costs by axing 450 jobs over the course of the year and shutting plants.

Underlying core earnings came in at €212m, down from €290m a year earlier. Mondi added that the Iran war had “further increased volatility in an already complex operating environment” leading to increased energy, raw material and logistics costs.

Reporting by Frank Prenesti for Sharecast.com

data.title: ZAWYA: Abu Dhabi International Boat show set to return in November 2026
data.description:
Abu Dhabi: The ultimate marine destination in the Middle East is set to return with the Abu Dhabi International Boat Show (ADIBS), scheduled to take place from 19–22 November 2026. Taking place at the Marina Hall, ADNEC Centre Abu Dhabi, this year’s event promises to bring together the global marine industry to showcase the latest innovations, luxury boats, and marine technologies.

Positioned at the start of the Gulf marine season, ADIBS provides exhibitors with the perfect opportunity to engage committed buyers at a time when demand is high and purchasing decisions are being made. With on-water trials, immersive experiences, and a returning Charter Show, the event offers brands a unique opportunity to connect directly with their audience, showcasing products in action and accelerating the decision-making process.

As the premier platform for the leisure marine industry in the region, ADIBS continues to attract the region’s most serious buyers, high-net-worth individuals, and business leaders, making it the undisputed commercial hub where business in the marine industry takes place. The 2026 edition will once again offer exhibitors and visitors a focused environment for networking and growth opportunities business, product launches, all at the start of the Gulf’s highly anticipated marine season.

ADIBS 2026 will once again host the region’s only dedicated Charter Show, providing a platform for exhibitors to showcase their yachts and luxury charter services to a growing regional audience. The Charter Show will feature an exclusive hospitality lounge, spotlighting premier showcases from leading international charter and brokerage companies.

As the region’s largest marine exhibition, ADIBS showcases the entire spectrum of the leisure marine industry, from luxury superyachts to sports boats and the latest marine products and services. With its proven track record, and unwavering government support, ADIBS delivers a unique competitive advantage for exhibitors and visitors alike.

Don't miss this chance to secure your prime location amongst leading brands at ADIBS 2026. Spaces are limited, and this is the opportunity to maximise visibility and sales in the region. To book your space today visit the ADIBS 2026 website here.

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com
